![][image1]

The following is a LinkedPost by Sohab Rahimi of Google. Find out more this solution. One of the biggest mistakes enterprises make with AI agents is treating every new use case as a new project. Legal builds a document analysis agent. Finance builds another. Customer service creates its own knowledge agent. Marketing develops a research agent. Engineering builds a planning agent while another team builds an almost identical one for IT. Different prompts. Different teams. Different code. Almost the same capability. The result is duplicated development, fragmented governance, inconsistent quality, and growing maintenance costs. Every improvement has to be implemented multiple times. The challenge isn’t building more agents. It’s scaling them without constantly rebuilding the same capabilities. The answer is composability. At Google Cloud Next ’26, we introduced 𝗔𝘁𝗼𝗺𝗶𝗰 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀 in Agent Garden. 𝗪𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝘀𝗰𝗿𝗶𝗯𝗲 𝗔𝘁𝗼𝗺𝗶𝗰 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀 𝗮𝘀 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻-𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗱𝘆, 𝗿𝗲𝘂𝘀𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗯𝗹𝗼𝗰𝗸𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗮𝗽𝘀𝘂𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗮 𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴𝗹𝗲 𝗯𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗰𝗮𝗽𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆. Atomic agents package proven prompting strategies, tools, orchestration, evaluation loops, and guardrails, allowing teams to assemble applications from trusted components instead of starting from scratch. For example, the SDLC blueprint is composed of three Atomic Agents: • User Story Refiner converts vague requirements into structured user stories with BDD acceptance criteria. • Technical Designer generates RFCs, ADRs, and architecture diagrams using the project’s code knowledge graph. • Task Planner decomposes the design into implementation tasks and execution plans. The same pattern appears in other blueprints, from on-brand media generation with automated quality evaluation to invoice processing with continuous rule learning. What I particularly like is how naturally this fits the agent mesh concept in the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform (GEAP). Instead of every workflow embedding its own planner, evaluator, researcher, or document processor, the platform can automatically invoke the appropriate Atomic Agent based on the required capability. The same production-hardened Planning Agent or Research Agent can serve dozens of applications. That shifts the unit of reuse from applications to capabilities. Build once. Improve once. Govern once. Reuse everywhere. I believe this is one of the most important architectural shifts for enterprise AI. Organizations that scale successfully won’t be the ones with the most agents. They’ll be the ones with the strongest library of reusable capabilities.  
Worked for 1m 28s

## **What Google’s “Atomic Agents” actually are**

**Atomic Agents are best understood as a productized capability-reuse pattern inside Google Cloud’s Agent Garden—not as a new type of model or a separate agent runtime.**

Google describes Agent Garden as a curated library of prebuilt, customizable agent samples and templates. Its Next ’26 material calls some of these templates “Atomic Agents” because each is intended to encapsulate a relatively narrow, reusable business capability that can be composed into larger multi-agent workflows. Google currently names examples such as code modernization, financial analysis, economic research, invoice processing, and retrieval-augmented generation.

The basic proposition is:

Stop constructing a new application-specific agent for every workflow. Build, evaluate, secure, deploy, and register reusable capabilities, then compose them into multiple business processes.

That is the architectural substance behind the post’s “build once, improve once, govern once, reuse everywhere.”

---

## **How the complete Google architecture fits together**

Atomic Agents only make sense as part of the larger **Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform**, which Google launched at Cloud Next ’26 as the successor and expansion of Vertex AI’s model and agent services. The platform is organized around build, scale, govern, and optimize.

### **1\. Agent Garden supplies the starting implementations**

Agent Garden contains source-accessible templates that can be configured, modified, and deployed. Google presently describes it as a **Preview** feature, subject to its pre-GA terms. The samples can be deployed through Agents CLI to Agent Runtime, embedded in a larger infrastructure template, or deployed and registered for use through Gemini Enterprise.

An Atomic Agent would therefore normally contain some combination of:

* Agent instructions and prompting strategies  
* Model configuration  
* Tool and data integrations  
* Control-flow or orchestration logic  
* Input/output structures  
* Guardrails  
* Evaluation assets  
* Observability instrumentation  
* Deployment configuration

That list is a synthesis of the surrounding platform rather than a formally published mandatory Atomic Agent manifest.

### **2\. ADK implements the internal behavior**

Google’s Agent Development Kit 2.0 provides the execution structure underneath these reusable capabilities. ADK 2.0 moved to a graph-based workflow engine in which agents, tools, functions, and deterministic nodes can participate in the same workflow graph. It supports:

* **Deterministic workflows** for specified execution paths  
* **Dynamic workflows** for code-controlled branching and iteration  
* **Collaborative workflows** involving coordinators and multiple subagents

ADK Python 2.0 became generally available on May 19, 2026, while ADK Go 2.0 became generally available on June 30, 2026\.

This is important because an Atomic Agent does not have to be “one prompt wrapped in an API.” It can be an internally structured workflow containing model reasoning, deterministic validators, tool calls, retries, and subordinate agents.

### **3\. Agent Runtime hosts the deployed capability**

Once customized, the agent can be deployed to Google’s managed Agent Runtime. Google says the current Runtime supports rapid provisioning, persistent sessions, Memory Bank integration, secure code or computer-use sandboxes, and agents that operate for multiple days.

Thus, a deployed Atomic Agent can become an independently operated service rather than merely a library imported into every application.

### **4\. Agent Registry makes the capability discoverable**

Agent Registry is the enterprise catalog for agents, MCP servers, tools, and endpoints. A registered agent becomes discoverable to developers and orchestrator agents. For A2A-compatible agents, the registry can read their Agent Cards and index their declared capabilities or “skills.”

ADK provides an Agent Registry client that can resolve registered endpoints at runtime. A parent orchestrator can retrieve a remote A2A agent and treat it as a subagent, or retrieve an MCP toolset and invoke those tools as part of its workflow.

That is the concrete mechanism behind the post’s “same Planning Agent or Research Agent can serve dozens of applications.”

### **5\. A2A connects agents; MCP connects tools**

Google’s architecture distinguishes two principal interoperability levels:

* **A2A:** an agent delegates a task to another agent that has its own reasoning, state, identity, and execution.  
* **MCP:** an agent invokes tools, APIs, data systems, or deterministic capabilities.

Agent Registry supports both patterns. Google explicitly positions A2A for agent-to-agent handoffs and MCP for tool and enterprise-system connectivity.

### **6\. Identity, Gateway, policies, and Model Armor govern usage**

Agent Identity gives deployed agents independently governed identities. Agent Gateway acts as the controlled traffic boundary between agents, tools, and data, applying authentication, authorization, policy enforcement, protocol mediation, and content-security protections. The Registry supplies the inventory and discovery layer.

This means “govern once” is intended to mean more than maintaining a shared prompt. A reusable capability can have centrally defined:

* Who may invoke it  
* Which data it may access  
* Which tools it may call  
* Which network destinations it may reach  
* Which semantic policies constrain its behavior  
* What telemetry and audit evidence it must produce

### **7\. Evaluation and observability support “improve once”**

Google’s optimization layer includes offline evaluation, simulated users, online monitoring, traces, topology views, prompt optimization, and reusable evaluation metrics. Its Metric Registry allows an organization to define a rubric or executable metric once and apply it to offline tests and continuous production monitoring.

Consequently, improving a shared Document Analyzer could theoretically improve every workflow that invokes the governed version—provided the organization has controlled versioning and compatibility practices.

---

## **Atomic Agent versus skill versus tool versus blueprint**

These terms are easy to conflate.

| Construct | Appropriate scope | Execution character |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Tool/MCP tool** | A bounded operation such as retrieving a record or creating a ticket | Predominantly deterministic |
| **Skill** | Instructions, code, and documentation that extend an existing agent | Loaded into the agent’s environment |
| **Atomic Agent** | An independently usable business capability with its own reasoning and operational envelope | Potentially autonomous and remotely invocable |
| **Blueprint** | A larger business workflow composed from agents, skills, tools, and control nodes | End-to-end orchestration |
| **Application** | User-facing or system-facing product delivering the outcome | Experience and business boundary |

Google’s separate **Skill Registry**, also currently in Preview, makes this distinction clearer. A skill is a self-contained package containing instructions, executable code, and documentation. Agents can dynamically discover and load relevant skills based on intent.

A sensible granularity rule would be:

* Use a **tool** when no independent reasoning is needed.  
* Use a **skill** when a capability should extend the current agent.  
* Use an **Atomic Agent** when the capability merits separate deployment, identity, scaling, evaluation, governance, or lifecycle management.  
* Use a **blueprint** when several capabilities must produce a business outcome together.

Not every reusable function should become a network-accessible agent.

---

## **What the SDLC blueprint is doing**

The example in the post appears to organize software planning into three reusable capabilities:

1. **User Story Refiner**  
    Converts underspecified intent into user stories and BDD acceptance criteria.  
2. **Technical Designer**  
    Produces RFCs, ADRs, and architecture representations using project knowledge.  
3. **Task Planner**  
    converts the approved design into sequenced implementation work and an execution plan.

The significant design decision is not merely the use of three agents. It is the creation of explicit artifact boundaries:

Intent  
  ↓  
Structured requirements contract  
  ↓  
Technical design contract  
  ↓  
Executable task plan

Each boundary can have its own schemas, validators, evaluation criteria, provenance, approval policy, and failure behavior. That is much closer to a compiler pipeline than to a conversational “team of personas.”

---

## **An important qualification about the “Agent Mesh”**

The illustration suggests that a workflow declares a needed capability and an Agent Mesh automatically selects the best Atomic Agent.

The currently public documentation supports most of the underlying mechanisms:

* Agents publish capabilities through A2A Agent Cards.  
* Agent Registry indexes them.  
* Registry supports keyword and prefix search.  
* ADK orchestrators can resolve registered endpoints dynamically.  
* Agents can delegate work through A2A.

However, I did **not** find public documentation for a separately exposed, turnkey “Agent Mesh” service with a documented ranking algorithm that automatically chooses the objectively best agent based on quality, cost, policy, latency, compatibility, and current health.

The post’s “auto-select” box therefore appears to be partly an architectural abstraction. An enterprise would still need a capability-resolution policy such as:

Required capability  
\+ input/output compatibility  
\+ authorization eligibility  
\+ approved version  
\+ domain applicability  
\+ quality score  
\+ latency and cost limits  
\+ regional/data-residency constraints  
\+ current availability  
────────────────────────────  
Selected capability instance

Registry search alone is not sufficient for high-assurance routing.

---

## **The principal architectural risks**

The core idea is sound, but reuse shifts rather than removes complexity.

### **Shared-upgrade blast radius**

Improving a widely reused agent may break many downstream workflows. Atomic Agents therefore need semantic versioning, compatibility tests, canary deployment, pinned versus floating dependencies, and rollback support.

### **Context leakage and domain dilution**

A generic Document Analyzer may perform poorly when “document analysis” means materially different things in legal discovery, invoice reconciliation, engineering specifications, and medical records. The reusable core must be separated from domain-specific policy, ontology, and evidence requirements.

### **Excessive atomization**

Too many networked agents create latency, token consumption, distributed failure modes, trace fragmentation, and ambiguous responsibility. Capability boundaries should follow cohesion and governance requirements—not an arbitrary desire to make everything an agent.

### **Probabilistic interface instability**

A reusable agent needs stronger contracts than a reusable software function because semantically plausible but incompatible output can still satisfy a JSON schema. Structural validation must be complemented by semantic invariants, evidence requirements, uncertainty thresholds, and downstream acceptance tests.

### **Selection becomes a control problem**

Once several agents claim the same capability, the platform must decide which one to trust. Capability matching, runtime admission, policy enforcement, quality history, cost, and escalation become part of the control plane.

---

## **What this confirms—and does not yet cover—for Janumi**

This development strongly validates your premise that the reusable enterprise unit should shift from the complete application to a **professional capability**.

The nearest Janumi mapping would be:

| Google concept | Possible Janumi counterpart |
| ----- | ----- |
| Agent Garden | Professional Capability Library |
| Atomic Agent | Reusable Professional Work Capability |
| Agent Registry | Professional Capability Registry |
| Skill Registry | Professional Method/Technique Registry |
| Agent Card | Capability Contract |
| ADK graph | Professional Work Architecture execution graph |
| Agent Gateway | Governed professional-work invocation boundary |
| Evaluation Registry | Validators, rubrics, invariants, and acceptance regimes |
| Agent blueprint | Reusable Professional Work Architecture |
| Agent Mesh | Capability discovery and resolution plane |

But Google’s solution is primarily an architecture for **deploying and reusing agent capabilities**. The Janumi conception goes beyond that when it adds:

* Professional Work Units with explicit outcome responsibility  
* Artifact and decision lineage  
* Cross-functional professional context  
* Invariants derived from policy and accumulated practice  
* Continuous reconciliation among evolving artifacts  
* Narrative memory and enterprise-state continuity  
* Escalation and tactic-change logic  
* A Living Enterprise Model

So Atomic Agents would fit **inside** the Janumi model. They would be reusable executors or capability providers employed by Professional Work Architecture. They would not replace the higher-order structure that determines what work must remain coherent, what changed, what conflicts, and what must be reconsidered.

My overall assessment is that Google has productized an important part of the architecture you have been developing: **capability-centered reuse, registry-based discovery, graph composition, common governance, and shared evaluation**. Janumi’s differentiated territory remains the control system above those capabilities—the system that continuously maintains professional coherence across outcomes, artifacts, organizational functions, and changing enterprise reality.

Would you like me to monitor Google’s release notes for Atomic Agents, Agent Garden, and Agent Registry changes as these Preview capabilities mature?
